title: One-step atmospheric microplasma synthesis of an NMC-type lithium-ion battery cathode
abstract_summary: The paper demonstrates an ultra-fast (less than one second) synthesis method for crystalline NMC-type lithium-ion battery cathode particles using a one-step atmospheric microplasma hollow-tube reactor. This process completely bypasses the conventional, energy-intensive calcination steps (which typically require temperatures exceeding 700 °C for hours).
green_strategy: Elimination of energy-intensive high-temperature calcination steps (>700 °C for hours) via ultra-fast, sub-second atmospheric microplasma synthesis.
fabrication_method: One-step atmospheric microplasma synthesis using a hollow-tube reactor

performance_metrics.condition: C/10 charge/discharge rate, 2.8–4.4 V vs. Li/Li+ in Gen-2 electrolyte (1.2 M LiPF6 in 3:7 wt:wt EC:EMC)
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: Typical commercial NMC cathodes exhibit higher capacity (e.g., 160-200 mAh/g) but require hours of calcination
Nanometer range typically observed in low/mid-vacuum plasmas

limitations: Inconsistent plasma discharge during synthesis (arc-like plasma instead of stable glow discharge).
Electrode sputtering/erosion causing iron contamination in the synthesized materials.
Exceedingly small product yield, making reliable and uniform material production challenging with the current setup.
Lower-than-expected initial discharge capacity (~125 mAh/g) compared to standard NMC cathodes due to low crystal quality and unreacted precursor.

figure_or_table_evidence.evidence_summary: Provides a schematic of the hollow-tube reactor setup (a) and cartoon of the reaction vessel (b) illustrating how the metal acetate liquid precursor vapor undergoes atmospheric plasma treatment.
SEM and EDX spectra demonstrating spherical particle morphology (0.1–3 µm) and confirming the elemental presence of Ni, Mn, and Co in the correct stoichiometric ratio of the NMC precursor.
XRD diffraction data indicating the products are at least partially crystalline hexagonal NMC-type phases before and after electrochemical cycling.

Shows discharge capacity over 10 cycles (a) starting around 125 mAh/g and declining to ~110 mAh/g, alongside dQ/dV plots (b, c) highlighting the active redox peaks resulting from the plasma-processed materials.

candidate_research_claims.claim_text: One-step atmospheric microplasma synthesis can produce crystalline, electrochemically active NMC-type lithium-ion battery cathode particles in less than one second without requiring an additional calcination step.
candidate_research_claims.experimental_condition: Hollow-tube microplasma reactor, Ar and O2 carrier gases, liquid acetate precursor vapor, 3000 V at 25 kHz
candidate_research_claims.limitation: Low discharge capacity (125 mAh/g) and contamination from electrode sputtering
candidate_research_claims.citation_text: This communication aims to highlight a nascent yet novel synthesis route: a one-step atmospheric microplasma process for synthesizing cathode particles in less than one second... all—without requiring an additional calcination step.
